item.title: Every Creepy Theory About The Pied Piper
item.author: Weird History
item.content:encoded:
A rainbow-clad stranger with a silver pipe saves a town from a rat infestation. But when that town stiffs him, he steals their children and vanishes into the hills, never to return. It sounds like a campfire story, but according to the people of Hamelin… it happened. The questions are: What happened?
To read more about the actual Pied Piper story, go here: https://www.ranker.com/list/theories-about-the-pied-piper-of-hamelin/elle-tharp
Be sure to subscribe to the Weird History Newsletter: https://bit.ly/WeirdHistoryNews
#piedpiper #germany #weirdhistory

item.title: This is Russia’s youngest political prisoner | DW Documentary
item.author: DW Documentary
item.content:encoded:
Arseny Turbin is one the youngest political prisoners in Russia. He was arrested in 2023, when he was just 14 years old. His crime, at such a young age? He criticized Russian President Vladimir Putin, in the classroom, on his YouTube channel, and in conversations with friends.
His teachers reported him to Russia's domestic intelligence agency, the FSB. Now 17, he’s serving a five year prison sentence. His mother, Irina drives over 400km every month to visit him at a juvenile prison in Moscow. She is fighting tirelessly for his release, terrified he may not make it out of the penal colony alive.
#documentary #dwdocumentary #dwdocs #Russia ______
We kindly ask viewers to read and stick to the DW netiquette policy on our channel: https://p.dw.com/p/MF1G

item.title: Trump vs. The World
item.author: Patrick Boyle
item.content:encoded:
👉🏻 To try everything Brilliant has to offer for free for a full 30 days, visit https://brilliant.org/patrick/. You’ll also get 20% off an annual premium subscription.
Last week, the White House decided to pivot from "America First" to "America Everywhere." It began on Saturday morning with a 150-aircraft military strike to serve what Secretary of State Marco Rubio described as a "routine law enforcement warrant"—proving once and for all that if you have a large enough aircraft carrier, every parking ticket is technically a tactical operation.
In this video, we look at the internal contradictions of the new "Donroe Doctrine," a policy that treats international borders as suggested boundaries and sovereign nations as distressed assets. We explore why the plan to "take the oil" faces a minor mathematical hurdle: according to Rystad Energy, 60% of Venezuela's production projects require an oil price of $80 per barrel just to break even. This makes the administration's plan to lower gas prices by flooding the market with oil a bit like trying to save money on your commute by buying a fleet of private jets.
We also look at the high-profile residents currently moving into the Metropolitan Detention Center in Brooklyn, why the "ultimate sin" in modern diplomacy is keeping your own Nobel Peace Prize, and why the Danish government is currently checking its lease agreement on Greenland after being told the U.S. is interested in a "hard way" to close the deal.

item.title: Inside Europe’s Economic Crises With Christine Lagarde | Leaders with Francine Lacqua
item.author: Bloomberg Originals
item.content:encoded:
Francine Lacqua speaks to Christine Lagarde about Europe’s economic travails, populism and how to lead through turbulent times. Lagarde, who served as managing director of the International Monetary Fund before becoming president of the European Central Bank, reflects on her response to the global financial crisis, the Eurozone debt crisis and bailout talks with Greece.
In a widely varied career, she spent more than two decades practicing corporate law before being recruited as France's trade minister in 2005. She sits down with Lacqua in Frankfurt to reveal how she handles high-pressure situations, complex negotiations and dramatic change.

item.title: The Political Assassinations That Changed The Course Of History
item.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
item.content:encoded:
Can an assassination change the course of history? Find out what led to the world's most significant assassinations and what impact they had on society. The assassinations of Archduke Franz Ferdinand, Rwandan President Juvénal Habyarimana, and Israeli Prime Minister Yitzhak Rabin have something key in common: these political assassinations triggered war, genocide and the deaths of countless others. When politically motivated assassins kill, the outcome is often the same: one death leads to many.

item.title: Dictators, drugs, discord - Latin America and the USA (3/3) | DW Documentary
item.author: DW Documentary
item.content:encoded:
To this day, the USA has a hand in almost all the crises affecting Latin America. Colombia, for example, sank into chaos in the 1990s. The cartels took over the country. People fled to the United States. Today, President Trump exploits this situation, using it to boost his popularity.
In Venezuela, Hugo Chávez’s victory in the 1998 presidential election sent shockwaves through the country. His radical reforms met resistance from Venezuela’s economic elites, who subsequently organised a coup. But in a dramatic turn of events, Chávez returned to power just 48 hours later. After his return, he blamed the United States for the attempted coup. In the 1990s, Colombia intensified its fight against drug trafficking with the help of the United States. The country was on the brink of collapse, as drug cartels and FARC guerrillas put increasing pressure on the government. With massive US support — and despite numerous human rights violations — President Álvaro Uribe implemented "Plan Colombia”, which was intended primarily to crack down on drug traffickers. But the drug routes simply shifted: Honduras became a new hub for cocaine. Because of ongoing human rights abuses, many Colombians left their country for the United States. Donald Trump exploited the resulting migration crisis, making it a central theme of his 2015 election campaign. Meanwhile, China stepped up its influence in Latin America. Panama’s President Juan Carlos Varela negotiated an agreement with Beijing without informing Washington, a long-standing ally.
Part 1: https://youtu.be/UkZASVWnHmU Part 2: https://youtu.be/IJ3zOE9V18I Part 3: https://youtu.be/tweCup3tL04

item.title: Dictators, drugs, discord - Latin America and the USA (2/3) | DW Documentary
item.author: DW Documentary
item.content:encoded:
Nearly all of Latin America’s crises have been shaped by the United States. In the 1980s, the US supported anti-communist forces in Nicaragua and the controversial strongman Manuel Noriega in Panama.
In the early 1980s, Panama avoided a civil war, but a shadowy figure came to power: Manuel Noriega, a long-time CIA agent and protégé of the United States. His main opponent, guerrilla leader Hugo Spadafora, accused Noriega of drug trafficking. In 1988, the US Department of Justice in Florida brought charges against Noriega, including for his links to Pablo Escobar's Medellín cartel. Noriega refused to step down, which led to the US invasion of Panama and his arrest. In Nicaragua, the Sandinistas introduced sweeping reforms after coming to power in 1979. Fearing the spread of a socialist revolution in Central America, the United States armed counter-revolutionary militias known as the Contras. A brutal guerrilla war followed. In Chile, ruler Augusto Pinochet faced growing opposition. In a 1988 referendum, the United States supported the democratic opposition through its ambassador, Harry Barnes. Pinochet was subsequently forced to step down. But another crisis was already unfolding: in 1989, the economic crisis in Venezuela, a traditional ally of the United States, reached its peak. Unrest broke out and was violently suppressed by the army.
Part 1: https://youtu.be/UkZASVWnHmU Part 2: https://youtu.be/IJ3zOE9V18I Part 3: https://youtu.be/tweCup3tL04

item.title: Dictators, drugs, discord - Latin America and the USA (1/3) | DW Documentary
item.author: DW Documentary
item.content:encoded:
Almost all Latin American crises of the 1960s and 1970s were influenced by the USA. Whether it was Brazil's dictatorship, the coup in Chile or the Cuban Missile Crisis - at some level, American politics were involved.
Brazil's first "national security" dictatorship was established in 1964 with the support of the United States. Washington, fearing an expansion of a perceived communist threat following the Cuban Revolution, rallied behind the coup leaders - making Brazil the first "national security" dictatorship in the region. Many opponents of the regime fled to Chile, where Augusto Pinochet seized power in a coup - also with the support of the United States. Almost simultaneously, the secret services of Chile, Brazil, Argentina, Bolivia, Uruguay and Paraguay joined forces under the code name "Operation Condor" to track down and eliminate their opponents. But in Nicaragua, a bloody civil war broke out and brought the leftist Sandinistas to power. This gave rise to a period of hope across the continent for greater independence from the United States. But Ronald Reagan's inauguration in 1981 marked a new era in relations with Latin America.
Part 1: https://youtu.be/UkZASVWnHmU Part 2: https://youtu.be/IJ3zOE9V18I Part 3: https://youtu.be/tweCup3tL04

item.title: This Is How You Get JARHEAD Sequels
item.author: Folding Ideas
item.content:encoded:
Clickbait Title: Annihilation 2: Absolution (2027) Ending Explained
I started work on this a year ago. The original script ended with a joke about touching grass, where I needed to go out into the snow and dig a little hole to find some grass to touch, and I even shot it as part of my "get hyped about the project" process. But then the Silkposting and CoD: Ghosts panels were approved for PAX East, so I had to pivot to working on those, and then I promised the final Ghosts video to PAX West as a live premiere, so Jarhead got kicked even further down the road, and then I got invited to a tour of Mr. Beast's studio which I absolutely couldn't turn down because I just could not figure out how they even got my name, so 2025 was a weird year.
Written by Dan Olson and Nathan Landel Edited by Dan Olson and Nathan Landel Performed by Dan Olson Executive Assistant Crystal Donovan
Special Thanks to Big Joel and my Patrons

He previously served as assistant U.S. attorney for the District of Maryland. The following interview was conducted by the Kirk Documentary Group's Mike Wiser for FRONTLINE on April 3, 2025. It has been annotated and edited for accuracy and clarity as part of an editorial and legal review. See a more complete description of our process here.

item.title: 1807-09: How The Peninsular War Began
item.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
item.content:encoded:
In 1807, Napoleon Bonaparte was the master of Europe. He expected the invasion of Portugal to be a short summer's campaign, but he instead triggered a Spanish Ulcer that would bleed his empire dry. This documentary explores the brutal beginnings of the Peninsular War, from the bloody Madrid uprising to Sir John Moore’s harrowing retreat to Corunna. Witness the true story behind the fictional world of Richard Sharpe and discover how a disorganized resistance and a small British force began the downfall of the world's most feared conqueror.

item.title: Java Generics and Collections • Maurice Naftalin & Stuart Marks
item.author: GOTO Conferences
item.content:encoded:
This interview was recorded for the GOTO Book Club. http://gotopia.tech/bookclub
Check out more here: https://gotopia.tech/episodes/406
Maurice Naftalin - Architect, Trainer & Co-Author of "Java Generics & Collections" Stuart Marks - Java & OpenJDK at Oracle & Technical Editor of "Java Generics & Collections"
RESOURCES Maurice https://bsky.app/profile/mauricenaftalin.bsky.social https://x.com/mauricenaftalin https://github.com/MauriceNaftalin https://www.linkedin.com/in/maurice-naftalin
Stuart https://bsky.app/profile/smarks.bsky.social https://mastodon.social/@stuartmarks https://x.com/stuartmarks https://github.com/stuart-marks https://www.linkedin.com/in/stuart-marks-17a71a2 https://stuartmarks.wordpress.com
DESCRIPTION Maurice Naftalin and Stuart Marks discuss the second edition of "Java Generics and Collections", published 19 years after the original. The conversation explores how Java programming has evolved from Java 5 to Java 25, covering major shifts like the move toward immutability, the introduction of sequenced collections, streams, and unmodifiable collections.
They delve into critical design topics including encapsulation of collections, the anemic domain model anti-pattern, the controversial unsupported operation exception, and the challenges of null handling. The book includes new chapters on usage guidance and design retrospectives that reflect decades of accumulated wisdom about the Collections Framework.
RECOMMENDED BOOKS Maurice Naftalin & Philip Wadler • Java Generics and Collections 2nd ed • https://amzn.to/47dOp9t Maurice Naftalin & Philip Wadler • Java Generics and Collections 1st ed • https://amzn.to/42JI03i Joshua Bloch • Effective Java • https://amzn.to/4oFbdoi Victor Grazi & Jeanne Boyarsky • Real-World Java • https://amzn.to/4oCEeBR Kevlin Henney & Trisha Gee • 97 Things Every Java Programmer Should Know • https://amzn.to/3kiTwJJ Christian Clausen • Five Lines of Code • https://amzn.to/3s2zjyg Nicolai Parlog • The Java Module System • https://amzn.to/3xFggR4

item.title: Why One Oil Company Has a Head Start in Venezuela
item.author: Bloomberg Originals
item.content:encoded:
With President Nicolás Maduro deposed, President Donald Trump wants US oil giants to revive Venezuela’s energy industry. Though it’s unclear who, if anyone, will take part in his plan, Chevron is already there.
After the US attack on the South American country and its rendition of President Nicolás Maduro, Trump said he wants to rebuild the country’s beleaguered infrastructure. While the oil majors mull his entreaties, Chevron may have a distinct advantage since it’s been operating in Venezuela for decades.

item.title: Declassified: The Lost H-Bombs Of The Cold War
item.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
item.content:encoded:
This is the full, declassified story of the Cold War's terrifying nuclear accidents. In 1966, a B-52 crash scattered plutonium over Palomares, Spain. Just two years later, another bomber crashed in Thule, Greenland, losing its payload beneath the Arctic ice. Discover how the US Air Force's risky "Broken Arrow" warning missions brought the world to the brink of accidental nuclear war. Through newly released documents and eyewitness accounts, we reveal the massive cleanup operations, the government cover-ups, and the enduring consequences of these lost H-Bombs.

item.title: The Value of Unexpected Results
item.author: StarTalk
item.content:encoded:
What is the goal of an experiment? Neil deGrasse Tyson and Chuck Nice break down the scientific process and why unexpected results are what make science so fascinating.
Timestamps: 00:00 - Introduction: Unexpected results 00:21 - The Scientific Process 02:30 - Designing an Experiment: Michelson–Morley 06:11 - Upending Expectations: Accelerating Universe & Number of Galaxies 10:55 - Closing

item.title: Historian Answers Google Questions About Rasputin
item.author: History Hit
item.content:encoded:
Supernatural Mystic? Russia's greatest love machine? Or one of history's greatest conmen responsible for the downfall of the Russian Royal Family? Whatever your opinion on the man from Siberia he certainly left a legacy.
Join historian Joshua Levine as he answers the internet's most asked questions about the one and only, Rasputin...
01:01 Was Rasputin real? 01:43 What was Rasputin’s real name? 02:14 What was Rasputin famous for? 03:32 Where was Rasputin from? 05:48 Was Rasputin a Priest? 07:49 How did Rasputin meet the Romanovs? 10:11 Coming Soon to History Hit 11:15 What was Rasputin’s job? 12:15 How did Rasputin heal Alexei? 13:52 Was Rasputin a fake? 15:36 What did Rasputin look like? 16:16 What was wrong with Rasputin’s eyes? 17:06 Did Rasputin smell? 17:36 Was Rasputin Charming? 18:16 When Was Rasputin by Boney M released?19:06 Was Rasputin Russia’s greatest love machine? 20:16 Did Rasputin have an affair with the Tsarina? 21:36 Why did Rasputin kill Anastasia? 23:06 How do you do the Rasputin dance? 22:56 Why did people not like Rasputin? 25:16 How did Rasputin die? 29:56 Did Rasputin cause the Russian Revolution? 30:48 What happened to Rasputin’s body? 30:56 Why is Rasputin still so famous?

item.title: Upstairs, downstairs: The life of a British maid - Stephanie Honchell Smith
item.author: TED-Ed
item.content:encoded:
Follow Alice Sutton, a servant in Edwardian England, as she prepares the estate for a visitor and fights for women's suffrage.
--
It’s just before dawn in 1906 at Wroxton Abbey, and 16-year-old Alice Sutton’s mind is already spinning. The underhousemaid has a full day ahead of her as the estate prepares for the evening’s guest: Prince Arthur. This is Alice’s chance to prove herself to her new employers — but there are other forces at play. Stephanie Honchell Smith details a day in the life of a servant in Edwardian England.
Lesson by Stephanie Honchell Smith, directed by Anton Bogaty.

item.title: The impact of industrial fishing in the Azores | DW Documentary
item.author: DW Documentary
item.content:encoded:
In the Azores, an autonomous region in Portugal, tuna fishing is done the way it has been for generations: one fisherman, one rod, one fish at a time. Nets are banned. Every catch is monitored and recorded.
But despite their efforts to keep fishing as selective and sustainable as possible, the Azores fishermen are struggling to make ends meet. Because of overfishing, the EU imposed fishing quotas that could run them out of business.
Local fishermen say they are frustrated because it’s not their fault the quotas have to be implemented. They say waters are depleted thanks to industrial fishing fleets casting out gigantic purse seine nets further out at sea, where they can easily evade monitoring.
#documentary #dwdocumentary #dwdocs #sustainablefishing #overfishing ______
We kindly ask viewers to read and stick to the DW netiquette policy on our channel: https://p.dw.com/p/MF1G

item.title: Hacking AI Systems: How to (Still) Trick Artificial Intelligence • Katharine Jarmul • GOTO 2025
item.author: GOTO Conferences
item.content:encoded:
This presentation was recorded at GOTO Copenhagen 2025. #GOTOcon #GOTOcph https://gotocph.com
Katharine Jarmul - Building Privacy-first Data & AI/ML Systems, Products & Platforms
RESOURCES https://twitter.com/kjam https://linkedin.com/in/katharinejarmul https://github.com/kjam https://kjamistan.com https://probablyprivate.com
ABSTRACT How easy is it to fool or trick today's AI systems?
In this talk, we'll wander through the field of adversarial AI/ML, looking at how attacks and AI systems have evolved over the past 10 years. You'll learn more about how deep learning works by investigating how, when and why it breaks and walk away with open security questions and some notebooks to keep learning and hacking! [...]

item.title: Archeologists Uncover Prisoner Remains At A Victorian Jail
item.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
item.content:encoded:
Join the Time Team as they excavate a modern police car park in Cumbria, England, which astonishingly holds the key to three different prison phases dating back to 1771. Discover the evolution of British crime and punishment, from communal Georgian cells for convicts awaiting transportation to the grueling solitary confinement of the Victorian era. We unearth evidence of an escape attempt, the women's prison, and the terrifying treadmill that killed a prisoner. To understand the true harshness, one of our team undergoes a brutal 24-hour Victorian prison regime.

item.title: The Electronic SA80: The EIW97 (Part Two) with firearms expert Jonathan Ferguson
item.author: Royal Armouries
item.content:encoded:
In this second part of What Is This Weapon, Jonathan and Murray return to the Electronic Individual Weapon 97 (EIW 97), an experimental SA80-based system developed in the 1990s to explore electronic fire control, programmable burst modes, and future soldier integration.
Following on from Episode 1, this episode focuses on how the weapon was programmed, tested, and demonstrated, using period-correct software, RS232 interfaces, and modern adapter hardware to bring the system back to life today.
Watch Episode 1 first for the historical background and mechanical overview.
00:03 Intro 01:04 Programming the weapon: trial laptop, software & RS232 interface 04:50 Adaptive fire control & tailoring rate of fire to the soldier 07:42 Power, sensors & internal diagnostics explained 10:04 Live software demonstration & weapon data readout 15:01 Burst modes, rate-of-fire settings & trial configurations 20:58 Demonstration setup: electronic blank firing attachment 31:00 Live demonstration of electronic firing modes 38:27 Service concerns, reliability & the ‘battery question’ 42:25 Future soldier systems, C4I & integrated weapons 46:44 Why electronic fire control still matters today 57:44 Final thoughts & preservation at the Royal Armouries
#RoyalArmouries #SA80 #L85A1 #L85A2 #L85A3 #SmallArms #BritishArmy #FirearmsHistory #WhatIsThisWeapon
Subscribe to our channel for more videos about arms and armour

We are the Royal Armouries, the United Kingdom's national collection of arms and armour. Discover what goes on behind the scenes and watch our collection come to life. See combat demonstrations, experience jousting and meet our experts.
Have a question about arms and armour? Feel free to leave us a comment and we'll do our best to answer it.

item.title: We've Finally Seen How Planets Form
item.author: Astrum
item.content:encoded:
This is HOPS-315. The James Webb Space Telescope has peered inside a cocoon of gas and dust, revealing a spectacular event for the first time ever. We’re witnessing the birth of a solar system not unlike our own, with proto-planets forming in real-time. Could this discovery finally unlock the secrets of our own solar system’s origin?
▀▀▀▀▀▀
0:00 How Solar Systems Are Born 5:13 How Do Planets Form? 6:26 HOPS-315 8:44 Baby Planets 9:46 Breaking the Models 12:24 What’s Next For HOPS-315?
▀▀▀▀▀▀

item.title: Why Are There No Holes Around Trees?
item.author: Kurzgesagt – In a Nutshell
item.content:encoded:
Go to https://brilliant.org/nutshell/ to dive deeper into these topics and more for free for a full 30 days + get 20% off the premium subscription! This video was sponsored by Brilliant. Thanks a lot for the support!
Get your LIMITED EDITION Golden Duck Pin here https://shop.kgs.link/golden and join us in celebrating 25 million subscribers! Thank you to each and every one of you for making this happen ❤️
Sources & further reading: https://sites.google.com/view/sources-treesii/
Trees are among the most fascinating life forms on Earth, but what makes them truly fascinating is hiding underground! They are able to break solid rocks with hydraulic roots, all while working together with the underground fungal networks that can stretch for kilometers. Above and below, trees are running biological empires we’re only beginning to understand.
How does something so massive survive with roots growing so shallow? What are trees really made of and how are they able to grow so big?

item.title: Massacre in El Salvador (full documentary) | FRONTLINE + ProPublica + RetroReport (Aired 2021)
item.author: FRONTLINE PBS | Official
item.content:encoded:
An investigation into the fight for justice after a deadly 1981 attack on the village of El Mozote and surrounding areas, and how the case against high-ranking military officials faltered. (Aired 2021)
This journalism is made possible by viewers like you. Donate to FRONTLINE now: https://bit.ly/47DFzCb
And support your local PBS station here: https://www.pbs.org/donate
This short documentary from FRONTLINE, Retro Report and ProPublica examined the horrors of what happened when U.S.-trained and equipped Salvadoran soldiers killed some 1,000 civilians, many of them children, in an attack more than 40 years ago. In the documentary, Raymond Bonner — who was one of the first journalists to bear witness to the aftermath of the massacre — recounted the experience of reporting the story and spoke with survivors of the attack.
This on-the-ground investigation detailed how, for decades following the tragedy, there was little movement towards holding anyone accountable, and how the case against high-ranking military officials faltered under Salvadoran President Nayib Bukele.
“Massacre in El Salvador” laid bare the nation’s grisly past and the decades-long struggle for justice.
“Massacre in El Salvador” is a FRONTLINE and Retro Report production with ProPublica. The producer and writer is Kit R. Roane. The senior producers are Frank Koughan and Molly Knight Raskin. The executive producer of Retro Report is Kyra Darnton.
Explore additional reporting on "Massacre in El Salvador" on our website: https://www.pbs.org/wgbh/frontline/documentary/massacre-in-el-salvador/
#Documentary #ElSalvador #ElMozote

item.title: Arnhem Aftermath: The Airborne Disaster That Changed The Course Of WW2
item.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
item.content:encoded:
Operation Market Garden was Field Marshal Montgomery's bold plan to end WW2 by Christmas. This documentary details the massive Allied gamble to seize bridges across the Netherlands, culminating in the tragic Battle of Arnhem. Discover why the core objective failed and how the elite British 1st Airborne Division, against impossible odds, fought a heroic but doomed four-day battle at the 'Bridge Too Far.' We examine the fatal flaws, from intelligence failures to poor communications, that turned a planned triumph into the Western Front's costliest failure.

item.title: Turkey: Can the Marmara sea be saved? | DW Documentary
item.author: DW Documentary
item.content:encoded:
Turkey's Sea of Marmara is on the brink of environmental collapse. Pollution, overfishing and climate change are depleting its oxygen. The crisis became visible when a thick layer of algal slime first began spreading across its waters in 2021.
Necla Köseoğlu, from Rumelikavağı, is one of the last people still working in traditional small-scale fishing on Turkey's Sea of Marmara. She's also one of the few women in the business. She used to be able to live off what she made from fishing, but those days are gone. Fish stocks in the Turkish inland sea have declined dramatically. The ecosystem is at risk of collapse due to overfishing, urban and industrial waste, and shipping pollution. The Marmara Sea, whose shores are home to around 25 million people, is suffocating. The crisis became widely known in 2021, when a kind of slime known as "sea snot" began to cover large parts of the sea's surface. The foul-smelling, gelatinous substance is an organic substance secreted by algae. It's not harmful to humans, but it starves other marine life of oxygen. Professor Hasan Örek is among the marine biologists and oceanographers sounding the alarm. Istanbul's city government has acknowledged the looming disaster. Environmental engineer Ayşen Erdinçler and her colleague Ilker Aslan say stricter controls and fines will curb pollution from shipping. But those measures are nothing more than a drop in the ocean. The greatest threat comes from millions of cubic meters of untreated domestic and industrial waste discharged into the sea each day. Local wastewater treatment plants urgently need modernization, but the Turkish government lacks the funds to carry it out.
#documentary #dwdocumentary #dwdocs #turkey #marmarasea ______

item.title: How Japan saved its biggest city from collapse - Roman Krznaric
item.author: TED-Ed
item.content:encoded:
Dig into Edo, Japan’s circular economy, and how it helped transform the city into a thriving ecological and cultural center.
--
400 years ago, Edo was on the verge of ecological collapse. Years of intense logging had led to massive deforestation and the city faced timber shortages, severe erosion, and frequent flooding. But over just a few decades, Edo became one of the most sustainable and efficient cities in history. So, how did this come about? Roman Krznaric explores Edo's transformation into a circular economy.
Lesson by Roman Krznaric, directed by Héloïse Dorsan-Rachet.

item.title: Building Better Software: Why Workflows Beat Code Every Time • Ben Smith & James Beswick
item.author: GOTO Conferences
item.content:encoded:
This interview was recorded for GOTO Unscripted. https://gotopia.tech
Check out more here: https://gotopia.tech/articles/407
Ben Smith - Staff Developer Advocate at Stripe James Beswick - Head of Developer Relations at Stripe
RESOURCES Ben https://twitter.com/benjamin_l_s https://github.com/bls20AWS https://linkedin.com/in/bensmithportfolio http://developeradvocate.co.uk https://thewebsmithsite.wordpress.com
James https://bsky.app/profile/jbesw.bsky.social https://twitter.com/jbesw https://linkedin.com/in/jamesbeswick
Links https://stripe.dev https://serverlessland.com
DESCRIPTION James Beswick and Ben Smith explore the evolution of modern software architecture. They discuss why workflow services are essential for managing distributed systems, the challenges of microservices versus monoliths, and the power of plugin architectures.
The conversation covers practical topics like idempotency, circuit breaker patterns, and the importance of observability, while also diving into what makes a great developer advocate and how to build demos that truly resonate with developers.

item.title: How Hitler Turned Bankrupt Germany Into A Military Machine
item.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
item.content:encoded:
June 1940: Hitler launches tanks and troops across France, Belgium and Holland. Yet Germany is impoverished, has few raw materials, and no oil or currency. How did the Nazis manage to set off the cataclysm of WWII with little money and a weak economy? In this episode we follow the period after World War I, where Germany faces a period of poverty. With industrial production at its lowest since their industrial revolution, the Nazi party dreams of setting the country back on a path to greatness through total war. Their first mission is to fund this goal.

item.title: This town wants more nuclear energy #shorts
item.author: Vox
item.content:encoded:
The town of Oswego, New York, has three nuclear power plants within 10 miles — and it wants another one. New York Gov. Kathy Hochul recently announced a commitment to add a gigawatt of nuclear power to the New York state grid, partially to meet the electricity demands of the booming AI industry. And Oswego is vying to be chosen as a site for this new power plant. To find out more about why Oswego is so eager for nuclear — and what would happen next if it’s chosen — Vox video producer Nate Krieger traveled there to meet with the mayor and see the nuclear power plants up close.
